fact: The first official Test match was played in 1877 between Australia and England at the Melbourne Cricket Ground. Australia won the match by 45 runs.
The inaugural Cricket World Cup was held in 1975 in England and was won by the West Indies, who defeated Australia in the final at Lord's.
India won the 2011 Cricket World Cup on home soil, defeating Sri Lanka in the final at Wankhede Stadium, Mumbai, with captain MS Dhoni hitting the winning six.
Sachin Tendulkar holds the record for the most international centuries in history, having scored 100 centuries across Test and One Day International cricket.
India won the 2024 ICC Men's T20 World Cup, beating South Africa by 7 runs in the final at Kensington Oval, Barbados, with Virat Kohli named Player of the Match for his 76 runs.
India won the 2025 ICC Champions Trophy, defeating New Zealand by 4 wickets in the final in Dubai to claim a record third Champions Trophy title while remaining undefeated throughout the tournament.
The Indian Premier League (IPL), a Twenty20 franchise cricket tournament, was founded in 2008 and is now one of the most-watched sporting leagues in the world.
A standard Twenty20 (T20) cricket match consists of a maximum of 20 overs per side, making it the shortest of the major international formats.
Test cricket is the longest format of the game, with matches traditionally scheduled to last up to five days.
AB de Villiers holds the record for the fastest century in One Day International cricket, reaching 100 runs off just 31 balls for South Africa in 2015.
Brian Lara holds the record for the highest individual score in Test cricket, scoring 400 not out for West Indies against England in 2004.
Sir Donald Bradman of Australia holds the highest Test batting average in history at 99.94, a figure widely regarded as one of the greatest statistical achievements in sport.
Australia has won the most Cricket World Cup titles, lifting the trophy six times as of 2024.
In cricket, a batter can be given out 'leg before wicket' (LBW) if the ball strikes their leg or body in line with the stumps and would otherwise have gone on to hit the wicket.
The first FIFA World Cup was held in 1930 in Uruguay, which hosted the tournament and also won the title by defeating Argentina in the final.
Brazil has won the FIFA World Cup a record five times, in 1958, 1962, 1970, 1994, and 2002.
Argentina won the 2022 FIFA World Cup in Qatar, defeating France on penalties after a 3-3 draw, with Lionel Messi finally winning the trophy that had eluded him.
Spain won UEFA Euro 2024, defeating England 2-1 in the final in Berlin, a result that gave Spain a record fourth European Championship title.
Paris Saint-Germain won their first-ever UEFA Champions League title in the 2024-25 season, defeating Inter Milan 5-0 in the final in Munich, the largest winning margin in a Champions League final.

The offside rule states that an attacking player is in an offside position if they are nearer to the opponent's goal line than both the ball and the second-last opponent when the ball is played to them.

Cristiano Ronaldo and Lionel Messi are widely regarded as two of the greatest footballers of all time, each having won multiple Ballon d'Or awards and scored over 800 career goals for club and country.
The United States won the first FIFA Women's World Cup in 1991, held in China, defeating Norway in the final.
The English Premier League was founded in 1992 when the top clubs broke away from the Football League to form a new top-tier competition.
Pele is the only player in history to have won the FIFA World Cup three times, with Brazil in 1958, 1962, and 1970.
A standard football (soccer) match consists of two 45-minute halves, for a total of 90 minutes of regulation play plus stoppage time.
Yellow and red cards were introduced to football at the 1970 FIFA World Cup in Mexico to help referees communicate cautions and dismissals more clearly.
The 1950 World Cup final match, in which host nation Brazil lost to Uruguay at the Maracana Stadium, is commonly known as the 'Maracanazo' and remains one of football's most famous upsets.
The Wimbledon Championships, first held in 1877, is the oldest tennis tournament in the world and the only Grand Slam still played on grass courts.
Tennis has four Grand Slam tournaments: the Australian Open, the French Open (Roland Garros), Wimbledon, and the US Open.
Novak Djokovic holds the record for the most men's Grand Slam singles titles in the Open Era, having surpassed 24 major titles by 2024.
Serena Williams won 23 Grand Slam singles titles during her career, the most of any player in the Open Era.
Jannik Sinner won the 2025 Wimbledon men's singles title, defeating two-time defending champion Carlos Alcaraz 4-6, 6-4, 6-4, 6-4, becoming the first Italian to win the Wimbledon singles crown.

Roger Federer, Rafael Nadal, and Novak Djokovic, often called the 'Big Three', dominated men's tennis for nearly two decades, collectively winning dozens of Grand Slam titles between 2003 and the early 2020s.
The tie-break, used to decide sets that reach 6-6, was introduced to tennis in 1970 to prevent excessively long sets.
Winning all four Grand Slam tournaments in the same calendar year is known as a 'Calendar Grand Slam,' a feat achieved by very few players in tennis history.
The Australian Open and US Open are played on hard courts, while the French Open is played on clay, giving each Grand Slam a distinct playing surface and style.
Wimbledon is famous for its strict all-white dress code for players, a tradition that dates back to the tournament's Victorian-era origins.
Carlos Alcaraz won the 2025 French Open men's singles title in an epic five-hour final against Jannik Sinner, extending his unbeaten run in Grand Slam finals at the time.

The longest match in tennis history was the first-round Wimbledon match between John Isner and Nicolas Mahut in 2010, which lasted over 11 hours across three days, with the final set alone finishing 70-68.

Badminton evolved from an earlier game called battledore and shuttlecock, and the sport's modern name is believed to derive from Badminton House in Gloucestershire, England, where the game was played in the 1860s.
The Badminton World Federation (BWF), the sport's global governing body, was founded in 1934 as the International Badminton Federation.
The Thomas Cup is the premier international men's team badminton championship, while the Uber Cup is its women's team equivalent; both are held biennially.
China won both the Thomas Cup and Uber Cup titles at the 2024 finals in Chengdu, defeating Indonesia in both finals and reclaiming the top position in world team badminton.
Badminton has been part of the Olympic Games as a full medal sport since the 1992 Barcelona Olympics.
PV Sindhu of India is the only Indian woman to win two individual Olympic medals in badminton, a silver at Rio 2016 and a bronze at Tokyo 2020.
A regulation badminton court net stands 1.55 meters high at the posts and 1.524 meters high at the center, and the court measures 13.4 meters long.
Lin Dan of China is the only male badminton player to win two Olympic gold medals in men's singles, achieving the feat at the 2008 Beijing and 2012 London Games.
Competitive badminton smashes have been recorded at speeds exceeding 400 km/h, making the shuttlecock one of the fastest-moving objects in any racquet sport.
China regained the top position in world team badminton in 2024 by winning the Sudirman Cup, Uber Cup, and Thomas Cup concurrently for the first time since 2012.
Basketball was invented in 1891 by Canadian-American physical education instructor James Naismith, who originally used peach baskets as goals.
The National Basketball Association (NBA) traces its origins to 1946, when it was founded as the Basketball Association of America (BAA).
Michael Jordan won six NBA championships with the Chicago Bulls in the 1990s and is widely regarded as one of the greatest basketball players of all time.
The United States men's basketball team won its fifth consecutive Olympic gold medal at the 2024 Paris Games, defeating host nation France 98-87 in the final.
At the 2024 Paris Olympics, Kevin Durant became the first men's basketball player to win four Olympic gold medals.
The NBA introduced the three-point line for the 1979-80 season, adding a new strategic dimension to professional basketball.
LeBron James surpassed Kareem Abdul-Jabbar to become the NBA's all-time leading scorer during the 2022-23 season.
The FIBA Basketball World Cup is the sport's premier international tournament for national teams, held every four years and organized by the International Basketball Federation.
In the NBA, teams must attempt a shot within 24 seconds of gaining possession of the ball, a rule known as the shot clock, introduced in 1954 to speed up the game.

The 1992 United States Olympic men's basketball team, nicknamed the 'Dream Team,' was the first US Olympic team to feature active NBA professionals and is considered one of the greatest sports teams ever assembled.
Field hockey has ancient origins, with stick-and-ball games recorded in ancient Egypt and Greece, though the modern rules of the sport were codified in England during the 19th century.
Field hockey has been part of the Olympic Games since the 1908 London Olympics for men, with a women's competition added in 1980.
India has won more Olympic gold medals in field hockey than any other nation, with eight titles won between 1928 and 1980.
The International Hockey Federation (FIH), founded in 1924, is the global governing body for field hockey.
India won the bronze medal in men's field hockey at the 2020 Tokyo Olympics, ending a 41-year wait since its last Olympic hockey medal in 1980.
The first Formula One World Championship season was held in 1950, with Italian driver Giuseppe Farina winning the inaugural Drivers' Championship driving for Alfa Romeo.
Michael Schumacher and Lewis Hamilton share the record for the most Formula One World Drivers' Championships, with seven titles each.
Max Verstappen won his fourth consecutive Formula One World Drivers' Championship in 2024, clinching the title at the Las Vegas Grand Prix with two races still remaining in the season.
Lando Norris won his first Formula One World Drivers' Championship in 2025, ending Max Verstappen's four-year reign as champion and securing the title at the season-ending Abu Dhabi Grand Prix.
The Monaco Grand Prix, held on the narrow street circuit of Monte Carlo, is considered one of the most prestigious and historic races on the Formula One calendar.
Usain Bolt of Jamaica set the current men's 100-meter world record of 9.58 seconds at the 2009 World Championships in Berlin.
The modern Olympic Games were founded in 1896 in Athens, Greece, reviving the ancient Olympic tradition after more than 1,500 years.
The marathon race distance of 42.195 kilometers is inspired by the legend of the Greek messenger Pheidippides, who is said to have run from Marathon to Athens to announce a military victory.
Florence Griffith-Joyner of the United States set the women's 100-meter world record of 10.49 seconds in 1988, a mark that has stood for decades.
The World Athletics Championships, first held in 1983, is the premier global competition for track and field events outside of the Olympic Games.
